title: Mental Health in Sport: What Every Athlete Should Know
summary: Recognize the mental health pressures that come with being an athlete, spot the signs a teammate is struggling, and learn how to connect them to help — including when it's a crisis.

sections.heading: Why mental health matters in sports
body: Sport builds toughness, teamwork, and confidence — but it also creates mental health pressures that are specific to being an athlete. Athletes are often taught to push through physical pain to keep playing. That same mindset can spill over into hiding emotional pain: an athlete who is anxious, exhausted, or down may stay quiet about it for the same reason they'd play on a sore ankle — they don't want to let the team, the coach, or their parents down.

For many athletes, being 'a [sport] player' isn't just an activity, it's part of their identity — how they see themselves and how others see them. That's often a source of pride and belonging. But it also means that when sport is taken away — by a bad season, being cut, or an injury — the loss can hit much harder than 'just missing practice.' It can feel like losing a piece of who they are.

A third pressure is burnout: physical and emotional exhaustion from too much training without enough rest, often made worse by specializing in one sport year-round starting at a young age. Burnout is linked to both higher injury rates and higher rates of anxiety and depression in young athletes.
list: Pressure to "play through it" — physically and emotionally — instead of reporting pain or how they're really feeling.
Identity fused with the sport, so a bad season, a benching, or an injury can feel like a threat to who they are, not just a setback.
Burnout from overtraining or single-sport specialization without rest, recovery, or cross-training.
Performing in public — mistakes happen in front of teammates, coaches, parents, and sometimes crowds, with immediate, visible consequences.

sections.heading: Recognizing anxiety and depression in a teammate
body: Anxiety is persistent, excessive worry or dread that doesn't go away — before a game, in the classroom, or with no clear trigger at all. It can show up physically too: a racing heart, stomachaches, or trouble sleeping the night before competition.

Depression is a persistent low mood or loss of interest in things a person used to enjoy, lasting weeks, not just a bad day. In an athlete, that often shows up first on the field or court, not in a conversation.

One rough practice or a snappy comment after a loss doesn't mean a teammate has anxiety or depression — everyone has off days. What matters is a pattern that persists over time and represents a real change from how that person normally is.
list: Withdrawal — pulling away from teammates, skipping team hangouts, going quiet in the locker room when they used to be talkative.
Noticeable mood or personality changes — more irritable, more on edge, or unusually flat and checked-out.

Declining performance paired with declining engagement — not just missing shots, but showing up late, not trying in drills, disengaging from huddles and teammates. Performance alone isn't the signal; performance plus pulling away is.
Talk that sounds hopeless — comments like "nothing matters" or "what's even the point," even said casually or as a joke.
Changes in sleep, appetite, or energy that last more than a couple weeks.

sections.heading: Burnout and disordered eating: recognizing the pattern
body: Burnout signs to watch for in a teammate: persistent fatigue that doesn't improve with normal rest, dreading practice or games they used to look forward to, and a loss of enjoyment in the sport even though they're still showing up and still trying.

Some athletes — especially in sports with weight classes, judged scoring, or an emphasis on leanness — develop disordered eating patterns tied to performance pressure or body image. This unit covers recognition only, at a peer level; the full physical picture (including RED-S, Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport) is covered where nutrition and energy availability are taught in depth. Your job as a teammate isn't to diagnose an eating disorder — it's to notice a pattern and tell a trusted adult.

Peer-level signs worth noticing: skipping meals or making rigid rules about what they'll eat, visible anxiety around eating in front of others, a noticeable weight change, or comments that fixate on weight or body size rather than performance.

sections.heading: The psychological side of injury and recovery
body: It is completely normal to feel frustrated, anxious, or down after a significant injury. Losing access to the team, the daily routine, and a source of identity all at once can trigger a grief-like response — similar to how people react to other major losses, not "just an injury." Athletes who seem more irritable, withdrawn, or low during a long recovery aren't overreacting; that's a normal response to a real loss.

Return to play isn't only about whether the tissue has healed. Fear of re-injury is a real and well-documented psychological barrier — an athlete can be medically cleared and still hesitate to cut, jump, or tackle the way they used to. That hesitation isn't weakness or a lack of toughness; it's a legitimate part of recovery that sometimes needs its own support, alongside the physical rehab.

A season-ending or career-ending injury is an even bigger loss and can be genuinely difficult to process. Athletes going through that may need support beyond what teammates or coaches can offer — connecting them to a school counselor or sports psychology professional is a normal, appropriate step, not an overreaction.

sections.heading: What a teammate can do
body: If you notice these signs in a teammate, you have a real role to play — but it's a specific and limited one. The same "recognize, protect, refer" pattern used for physical injuries applies here: recognize the signs, support them in the moment, and connect them to someone qualified to help.
list: Listen without judging. Let them talk without interrupting, minimizing ("it's not that bad"), or jumping straight to advice.
Don't try to diagnose or fix it. You don't need to know if it's "anxiety" or "depression" or have a solution — noticing something is wrong is enough.
Know it isn't your job to be their therapist. Being a supportive friend and being a mental health professional are different roles. Carrying that weight alone isn't fair to you or safe for them.
Connect them to a trusted adult — a coach, athletic trainer, school counselor, or parent. Offer to go with them the first time if that helps.

image.description: Two teammates in practice gear sitting together on a bench off to the side of a field or court, one leaning in and listening attentively with open, calm body language while the other talks. Warm, low-key scene with no text, no visible score or crowd — the focus is entirely on the supportive one-on-one conversation. Simple, gentle illustration style, nothing clinical or distressing.
image.alt: Illustration of one teammate listening supportively while another talks, depicting a peer-support conversation.

callout.title: Starting the conversation
callout.text: You don't need a perfect script. Something as simple as "Hey, you've seemed off lately — how are you actually doing?" opens the door. If they open up, listen first; you can ask together, "Who's a good adult to loop in on this?" once they're ready.

sections.heading: When it's a crisis: talk of suicide or self-harm
body: Sometimes what a teammate shares is more serious than everyday stress. If someone talks about suicide, self-harm, or not wanting to be alive — even if it sounds vague, indirect, or like a joke — treat it as serious every single time. You are never wrong to take it seriously and get help.

callout.title: If a teammate mentions suicide or self-harm — act immediately
callout.text: Take every mention seriously, no exceptions. Do not promise to keep it a secret, even if they ask you to — this is one situation where keeping a friend's secret can cost their life. Stay with them if you safely can, and get an adult (coach, athletic trainer, school counselor, parent, or call 911 if there's immediate danger) right away. A crisis line — a phone or text service staffed by trained counselors, available any time — is also a real resource for you or your teammate. Getting an adult involved fast is not betraying your friend; it's protecting them.

sections.heading: Destigmatizing: seeking help is strength
body: Sports culture has historically treated mental health as something to push through in silence, the same way athletes were once told to "walk off" injuries that needed real treatment. That mindset is changing. In recent years, elite athletes across many sports have spoken publicly about anxiety, depression, and stepping back from competition to protect their mental health.

Seeking help — from a coach, a school counselor, a parent, or a mental health professional — is not a sign of weakness or a lack of commitment to the team. It's the same kind of smart, proactive move as reporting a physical injury early instead of playing through it and making it worse.

quiz.question: Why might an athlete hide how they're really feeling, emotionally, from teammates and coaches?
choices: The same pressure that pushes athletes to play through physical pain — not wanting to let the team down
Because coaches are legally required to bench any athlete who admits to stress
Because talking about feelings always makes performance worse
Because it's against most team rules to discuss emotions
quiz.explanation: The pressure to 'play through it' applies to emotional struggles just as much as physical pain — athletes often stay quiet to avoid feeling like they're letting the team down. The other options describe rules or effects that don't exist.

quiz.question: A teammate has been quieter than usual, snaps at people more, and has stopped trying in drills for the past three weeks — a real change from how they normally act. What does this pattern most likely signal?
choices: Nothing worth mentioning — everyone has an off game sometimes
A possible mental health struggle worth telling a trusted adult about
That they've decided to quit the team and should be left alone
That they need a stricter practice schedule to refocus
quiz.explanation: A single bad practice is normal, but a persistent pattern of withdrawal, mood change, and declining engagement over weeks is exactly the kind of signal covered in this unit — worth telling a trusted adult about, not ignoring or trying to fix yourself.

quiz.question: Which combination of signs best describes burnout in an athlete?
choices: One missed practice due to a family event
Persistent fatigue that doesn't improve with rest, dreading practice, and loss of enjoyment in a sport they used to love
Being nervous before every single game they play
Wanting extra reps after practice to improve a weak skill
quiz.explanation: Burnout is a pattern: ongoing exhaustion that rest doesn't fix, dread instead of excitement, and lost enjoyment despite still showing up. Occasional nerves before competition or wanting extra practice are normal and not burnout on their own.

quiz.question: An athlete with a season-ending ACL tear seems irritable and withdrawn from the team weeks into recovery. What's the best way to understand this?
choices: They're being overly dramatic about a physical injury
This is a normal grief-like response to a real loss — routine, team connection, and identity — and support may help
This means the injury must be more physically severe than diagnosed
Nothing can be done until they are medically cleared to play again
quiz.explanation: A serious injury takes away more than participation — it can take away routine, team connection, and a piece of identity, which is why a grief-like emotional response is normal and expected, not an overreaction. Support (like a counselor or athletic trainer) can help during recovery, not just after clearance.

quiz.question: A teammate confides that they've been skipping meals and feel anxious about an upcoming weigh-in. What should you do?
choices: Give them a meal plan you found online to help them make weight safely
Tell them to just eat normally and stop worrying about it
Listen without judgment, and connect them with a coach, athletic trainer, or school counselor
Keep it private since it's their personal choice and not your business
quiz.explanation: Disordered eating patterns need a qualified professional, not peer nutrition advice or dismissal. Listening without judgment and routing them to a trusted adult follows the same recognize-and-refer pattern used for physical injuries.

quiz.question: A teammate tells you, 'Sometimes I think everyone would be better off without me,' and asks you not to tell anyone. What do you do?
choices: Keep the secret since they specifically asked you to and trust is important
Wait a few days to see if their mood improves before deciding what to do
Take it seriously right away, don't promise secrecy, and get a trusted adult involved immediately
Tell them to talk to a counselor themselves whenever they're ready
quiz.explanation: Any mention of not wanting to be alive is treated as serious every time, with no waiting period. Promising secrecy on this topic can cost a life, so the right move is staying with them if safe and getting an adult involved immediately.

quiz.question: An athlete is medically cleared to return from injury but still hesitates during cutting and jumping drills. What does this most likely reflect?
choices: A lack of toughness or commitment to the team
Fear of re-injury, a real and legitimate psychological part of recovery, not weakness
Proof that the injury was never actually healed
A sign they should be immediately removed from the team
quiz.explanation: Fear of re-injury is a well-documented, legitimate psychological barrier that can persist even after full medical clearance. It reflects the psychological side of recovery, not a lack of toughness, and often benefits from its own support alongside physical rehab.

quiz.question: Which statement best reflects how sports culture is shifting on mental health?
choices: Athletes who ask for mental health support are less committed to their team
Mental health struggles should be handled privately and never discussed
Seeking help is a sign of strength, and many elite athletes have spoken publicly about their own struggles
Only athletes with a diagnosed disorder should ever talk to a counselor
quiz.explanation: Seeking help is increasingly recognized as a proactive, smart move — the same logic as reporting a physical injury early — and many elite athletes have spoken openly about mental health, helping reduce stigma around asking for support.

flashcards.back: How much a person's sense of self is tied to being an athlete. A normal source of pride, but it means injury or being sidelined can feel like a threat to who they are, not just a schedule change.
Persistent fatigue that doesn't improve with rest, dread of practice or games they used to enjoy, and loss of enjoyment in the sport despite still trying.
The peer-support version of 'recognize, protect, refer': notice the signs, be supportive in the moment, and connect them to a trusted adult qualified to help.
A real, legitimate psychological barrier that can persist even after full medical clearance — hesitation during return to play, not a sign of weakness.
Persistent, excessive worry or dread; can show up physically as racing heart, stomachaches, or trouble sleeping before competition.
Persistent low mood or loss of interest lasting weeks, not just a bad day; often shows up first as withdrawal and disengagement in sport.
Skipping meals, rigid food rules, anxiety around eating in front of others, noticeable weight change, or fixation on weight/body in conversation.

flashcards.front: If a teammate mentions suicide or self-harm
flashcards.back: Take it seriously every time, don't promise secrecy, stay with them if safe, and get an adult (or call 911 for immediate danger) right away.

flashcards.front: What NOT to do as a peer
flashcards.back: Don't diagnose, don't try to fix it yourself, and don't promise to keep a crisis-level disclosure secret. Supportive friend and mental health professional are different roles.

flashcards.front: Trusted adults to connect a struggling teammate with
flashcards.back: Coach, athletic trainer, school counselor, or parent — any adult positioned to get them real support.
Frustration, anxiety, or low mood after losing routine, team connection, and identity all at once — a normal reaction to a real loss, not overreacting.
Seeking mental health support is a sign of strength, not weakness — comparable to reporting a physical injury early. Many elite athletes have spoken publicly about their own mental health.
